payload.formula_text: Assist Ratio = Assisted Conversions / Last-Click Conversions
payload.formula_explanation: Compares conversions where a channel was the final touchpoint (last-click) versus conversions where it assisted earlier in the journey. High assist ratios indicate channels that initiate journeys rather than close them.
how_to_read: Green bars show last-click conversions (channel closed the deal)
Light green bars show assisted conversions (channel was in the journey)
Channels with more assists than last-clicks are "introducers"
Channels with more last-clicks are "closers"
Paid Social often has high assist ratios
Brand Search typically has high last-click ratios
decisions_answered: Which channels start customer journeys vs close them?
Is Paid Social being undervalued by last-click attribution?
Should we give more credit to upper-funnel channels?
How do channels work together in the conversion path?
payload.narration_text:
This chart compares two types of conversions for each channel.

Green bars are last-click conversions - where the channel got the final credit. Light green are assisted conversions - where the channel touched the customer earlier in their journey but someone else closed.

Channels with more assists than last-clicks are introducers - they start journeys. Paid Social often falls here. Channels with more last-clicks are closers - they finish journeys. Brand Search typically dominates here.

This matters for budgeting. Last-click attribution undervalues introducers. If a channel shows high assists, it may deserve more investment than last-click ROAS suggests.

payload.formula_text: Daily Gap % = ((Platform Reported - GA4 Tracked) / Platform Reported) × 100
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks how the attribution gap percentage changes over time for Google and Meta. Increasing gaps may indicate tracking degradation or changing user behavior.
how_to_read: Blue line shows Google Ads gap percentage over time
Purple line shows Meta Ads gap percentage over time
Higher values indicate larger discrepancy from platforms
Spikes may indicate tracking issues or unusual campaign activity
Trend direction shows whether attribution is improving or degrading
Use crosshair to see exact values for any date
decisions_answered: Is the attribution gap getting better or worse over time?
When did significant changes in attribution occur?
Which platform has more volatile attribution?
Should we investigate recent tracking changes?
payload.narration_text:
This chart tracks how attribution gaps change over 90 days.

The blue line is Google Ads gap percentage. The purple line is Meta Ads. Higher percentages mean platforms are claiming more than GA4 confirms.

Watch for trends. A rising line means the gap is worsening - maybe tracking degraded or user behavior changed. A falling line means improvement.

Spikes deserve investigation. They could indicate tracking issues, iOS changes, or unusual campaign activity.

This helps you monitor: Is attribution accuracy improving? When did problems start? And which platform needs closer attention?

payload.formula_text:
Platform Self-Reported: What Google/Meta claim as conversion revenue
Last-Click (GA4): 100% credit to final session source
First-Click (GA4): 100% credit to initial user acquisition source
Linear (Approx): Average of First-Click and Last-Click revenue
Time Decay (Approx): Last-Click × 0.7 + First-Click × 0.3
Position-Based (Approx): First-Click × 0.5 + Last-Click × 0.5
payload.formula_explanation: Each attribution model assigns conversion credit differently. Note: Linear, Time Decay, and Position-Based are approximations â€” GA4 only provides aggregated first-touch and last-touch data at the channel level, not user-level click journeys needed for true multi-touch attribution. These models blend first-click and last-click revenue using weighted averages as a practical substitute.
how_to_read: Compare Platform Self-Reported vs GA4 columns to see attribution gap
Green values indicate strong performance relative to spend
Red highlighting shows gaps greater than 30%
Channels with higher First-Click vs Last-Click are "introducers"
Channels with higher Last-Click vs First-Click are "closers"
Sort by any column to rank channels on that model
decisions_answered: How does each channel perform under different attribution models?
Which channels introduce customers vs close deals?
What is the attribution gap for each advertising platform?
Which attribution model should we use for budget decisions?
Should we reallocate budget based on first-touch contribution?
payload.narration_text:
This table compares how revenue is attributed under six different models.

Platform Self-Reported shows what Google and Meta claim. Last-Click and First-Click come directly from GA4 data. Linear, Time Decay, and Position-Based are approximations â€” since GA4 only provides aggregated first-touch and last-touch data, not user-level journeys, these models blend first-click and last-click revenue using different weightings as a practical substitute for true multi-touch attribution.

Compare the columns to see which channels are "introducers" - high in first-click - versus "closers" - high in last-click.

The gap between Platform and GA4 reveals how much platforms over-claim. Use this to decide which attribution model best fits your business for budget allocation.

payload.formula_text:
Estimated Category = Direct Revenue × Estimated Attribution %
Recoverable Revenue = Categories that could be attributed with proper tagging
payload.formula_explanation: Estimates what is hidden inside the "Direct" traffic bucket. True Direct traffic is bookmarks and typed URLs. The rest is likely untracked email, social, or paid traffic that lost attribution.
how_to_read: Pie chart shows estimated breakdown of Direct traffic sources
Gray slice is "True Direct" - actual bookmarks and typed URLs
Purple slice estimates email clicks without UTM parameters
Red slice estimates paid traffic with broken UTM tags
Dark gray is "Dark Social" - private messages and shares
Green insight box shows potential recoverable revenue
decisions_answered: What is really hiding in our Direct traffic?
How much revenue could we attribute with better tracking?
Which untracked source is leaking the most attribution?
Should we prioritize UTM tagging improvements?
payload.narration_text:
The Direct Traffic Black Hole breaks down what is hiding in unattributed traffic.

Not all Direct traffic is true direct - people typing your URL or using bookmarks. Much of it is tracking leakage from other channels.

The pie chart estimates: True Direct in gray, untracked email clicks in purple, broken paid UTMs in red, and dark social - private messages and shares - in dark gray.

The insight box shows how much revenue you could recover with proper UTM tagging.

This answers: What is really in your Direct bucket? How much attribution are you losing? And is UTM improvement worth the investment?

payload.formula_explanation: Percentage of total revenue attributed to "Direct" traffic - visitors with no tracked referral source. High Direct share often indicates tracking gaps, untagged campaigns, or dark social traffic that cannot be attributed.
how_to_read: Percentage shows how much revenue has no source attribution
Below 30% is healthy - most traffic is properly attributed
Between 30-40% (amber) indicates potential tracking issues
Above 40% (red) signals significant attribution gaps to investigate
High Direct share may hide email, social, or paid traffic
decisions_answered: How much revenue is unattributed to any marketing channel?
Are we losing attribution due to tracking issues?
What portion of Direct might actually be other channels?
Should we invest in better UTM tagging and tracking?
payload.narration_text:
Direct Traffic Share shows what percentage of revenue has no marketing attribution.

Direct traffic means visitors who typed your URL directly, used bookmarks, or - more often - came from sources that lost their tracking parameters.

Below 30% is healthy. Between 30 and 40% suggests potential tracking issues. Above 40% means significant revenue is hiding in the Direct "black hole."

This matters because: High Direct share often hides email clicks, dark social shares, or paid traffic with broken UTM tags. Understanding this helps you know whether to invest in better tracking.

payload.formula_text:
Gap = Google Ads Self-Reported Revenue - GA4 Last-Click Revenue
Gap % = (Gap / Google Self-Reported) × 100
payload.formula_explanation: The difference between what Google Ads claims as conversion value and what GA4 last-click attribution tracks. This gap arises from view-through conversions, cross-device tracking, and different attribution windows.
how_to_read: Dollar amount shows the absolute gap between platform and GA4
Percentage indicates how much Google may be over-reporting
Amber color (30-50%) indicates moderate discrepancy - investigate
Red color (>50%) indicates high discrepancy - action required
Gap below 30% is considered acceptable
decisions_answered: How much is Google over-claiming in conversion value?
Should we trust Google Ads reported ROAS?
Is the attribution gap widening or narrowing over time?
Do we need to adjust bidding strategies based on true performance?
payload.narration_text:
The Google Attribution Gap shows the difference between what Google Ads claims versus what GA4 actually tracks.

Google self-reports its conversions, often including view-through attribution and cross-device conversions that GA4 doesn't capture.

A gap under 30% is acceptable. Between 30 and 50% - shown in amber - warrants investigation. Above 50% - shown in red - requires action.

This helps you understand: How much should you trust Google's reported ROAS? And should you adjust your bidding strategies based on true GA4 performance?

payload.formula_text:
Gap = Meta Ads Self-Reported Revenue - GA4 Last-Click Revenue
Gap % = (Gap / Meta Self-Reported) × 100
payload.formula_explanation: The difference between Meta (Facebook/Instagram) reported conversions and GA4 last-click. Meta typically shows higher gaps due to 7-day click and 1-day view attribution windows that GA4 last-click model does not capture.
how_to_read: Dollar amount shows the absolute gap between Meta and GA4
Meta gaps are typically higher than Google (60-80% common)
Amber color (50-75%) indicates expected Meta behavior
Red color (>75%) indicates unusually high discrepancy
Compare to Google gap to understand relative platform behavior
decisions_answered: How much is Meta over-claiming in conversion value?
How does Meta attribution compare to Google attribution?
Should we rely on Meta reported metrics for budget decisions?
Is Meta receiving more or less credit than it deserves?
payload.narration_text:
The Meta Attribution Gap shows the difference between what Meta claims versus GA4 tracking.

Meta typically has higher gaps than Google because of their 7-day click and 1-day view attribution windows. GA4's last-click model is more conservative.

A gap between 50 and 75% is normal for Meta. Above 75% is unusually high.

This answers critical questions: How much is Meta over-claiming? Should you trust their reported ROAS? And is Meta receiving appropriate credit compared to other channels?

payload.formula_text: Total Revenue = SUM(GA4 TOTAL_REVENUE) for all sessions in date range
payload.formula_explanation: Sum of all GA4-tracked revenue across all traffic sources and channels in the last 90 days. This is the baseline for understanding overall marketing performance.
how_to_read: Large number shows total revenue attributed by GA4 last-click model
Sparkline shows recent daily revenue trend
Green indicates revenue is up vs previous period
Red indicates revenue is down vs previous period
decisions_answered: What is our total trackable revenue for the period?
Is overall revenue trending up or down?
How does current performance compare to previous 90 days?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI shows your total GA4-tracked revenue over the last 90 days.

The formula sums all revenue from Google Analytics 4 across all traffic sources - paid, organic, direct, and referral.

The sparkline shows your daily revenue trend. Green means you're up versus the previous period. Red means you're down.

This answers the fundamental question: What is our total trackable revenue? And is it trending in the right direction?

payload.formula_text: Path Length = Number of marketing touchpoints before conversion
payload.formula_explanation: Shows how many marketing interactions customers have before purchasing. Single-touchpoint conversions benefit last-click attribution. Multi-touch journeys require more sophisticated attribution models.
how_to_read: X-axis shows number of touchpoints (1, 2, 3, 4, 5+)
Bar height indicates percentage of conversions at that path length
High single-touchpoint rate favors simpler attribution
High multi-touch rate suggests need for multi-touch models
Average path length shown as annotation
Longer paths typically have higher order values
decisions_answered: How complex are our customers purchase journeys?
Do most customers convert in one touch or many?
Is last-click attribution appropriate for our business?
Should we invest in nurture sequences for longer journeys?
payload.narration_text:
Path Length Distribution shows how many marketing touchpoints customers have before converting.

If most conversions happen in a single touch, last-click attribution works fine. If customers need multiple touches, you need multi-touch attribution models.

Look at the bar heights. High bars on the left mean simpler journeys. High bars on the right mean complex, multi-touch paths.

The average path length matters too. Longer paths often correlate with higher order values - customers who research more spend more.

This helps you understand: Is your purchase journey simple or complex? And does your attribution model match reality?

payload.formula_text: Flow Width = Revenue volume flowing from First-Touch Channel to Last-Touch Channel
payload.formula_explanation: Visualizes how customers journey from their first marketing touchpoint to their final converting touchpoint. Wider flows indicate more revenue following that path.
how_to_read: Left side shows first-touch channels (how customers discovered you)
Right side shows last-touch channels (where customers converted)
Flow width represents revenue volume following that path
Same-channel flows indicate single-touch or brand loyalty
Cross-channel flows show customer journey complexity
Hover for exact revenue values on each flow
decisions_answered: How do customers flow from discovery to conversion?
Which channels are primarily introducers vs closers?
What cross-channel paths generate the most revenue?
Should we invest more in channels that initiate journeys?
payload.narration_text:
This Sankey diagram shows how revenue flows from first-touch to last-touch channels.

The left side is where customers first discovered you. The right side is where they finally converted. Flow width indicates revenue volume.

When revenue flows between different channels, it shows journey complexity. When it stays in the same channel, customers converted in a single touch or returned to familiar channels.

This answers: How do customers flow through your marketing? Which channels start journeys versus close them? And where should you invest to capture more revenue at different journey stages?

payload.formula_text: Time to Convert = Days from first marketing touch to purchase
payload.formula_explanation: Shows how long customers take from first marketing interaction to purchase. Same-day conversions are impulse buys. Longer windows require nurturing and may lose attribution to platform cookie windows.
how_to_read: X-axis shows time buckets (Same Day, 1-3 Days, etc.)
Bar height indicates percentage of conversions in that window
Same-day conversions are typically lower-funnel or retargeting
Longer windows often involve upper-funnel discovery channels
Compare to platform attribution windows (7-day, 28-day)
Conversions beyond window may lose platform attribution
decisions_answered: How quickly do customers convert after first touch?
Are we losing attribution to expired cookie windows?
Should we adjust platform attribution window settings?
What is the typical consideration period for our products?
payload.narration_text:
Time to Conversion shows how long customers take from first marketing touch to purchase.

Same-day conversions are typically impulse buys or retargeting success. Longer windows indicate customers who need time to research or consider.

Compare your distribution to platform attribution windows. If many customers take more than 7 days, Meta's default 7-day click window may miss conversions. If more than 30 days, even Google's 30-day window might not capture the full journey.

This answers key questions: What is your typical sales cycle? Are platforms cutting off attribution too early? And should you extend attribution windows to capture delayed conversions?

payload.formula_text: VTC Share % = View-Through Conversions / (Click Conversions + View-Through Conversions) × 100
payload.formula_explanation: View-through conversions are when someone sees an ad but does not click, then converts later. High VTC ratios can inflate platform-reported performance since these are harder to attribute definitively.
how_to_read: Blue portion shows click-through conversions (more reliable)
Yellow portion shows view-through conversions (less reliable)
Campaigns with high yellow are claiming view-through credit
PMAX campaigns typically show highest VTC ratios
Brand campaigns usually show minimal or zero VTC
High VTC may explain platform vs GA4 attribution gap
decisions_answered: Which campaigns claim the most view-through credit?
How much of platform-reported revenue is from VTC?
Should we adjust VTC attribution windows in platform settings?
Are certain campaign types over-relying on view-through claims?
payload.narration_text:
This chart shows how much each campaign relies on view-through conversions versus click conversions.

Click conversions - in blue - are more reliable. Someone clicked your ad, then converted. View-through conversions - in yellow - mean someone saw your ad, didn't click, but converted later. Platforms claim credit, but it's harder to prove causation.

Performance Max campaigns typically show the highest VTC ratios. Brand search campaigns usually have none.

High VTC explains why platforms report higher ROAS than GA4. If a campaign is mostly yellow, be skeptical of its reported performance. Consider adjusting your VTC attribution windows.
